steps.title: Identify the structure of the sentence
steps.content: "Hoping to help her students and their families outside the classroom" is an introductory participial phrase modifying Huerta. What follows — "Huerta left teaching to start the Stockton chapter..." — is the main independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content: Standard English requires a comma after an introductory modifying phrase to separate it from the independent clause it modifies.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — classroom.: a period creates a sentence fragment out of the introductory phrase, which has no subject or verb of its own. Violates fragment rule. ✗
  • Choice B — classroom;: a semicolon must join two independent clauses, but the first half is only a participial phrase, not a clause. Violates semicolon rule. ✗
  • Choice C — classroom,: a comma is exactly what an introductory modifier requires before the main clause. ✓
  • Choice D — classroom: omitting punctuation runs the introductory phrase into the main clause, violating the rule that requires a comma after an introductory modifier. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
